name: Yoruba
description: The Yoruba are one of the largest ethnic groups in Africa, with a diaspora that spans the Americas, Caribbean, and Europe through the transatlantic slave trade. Their civilisation traces back to the ancient city of Ile-Ife, considered the birthplace of humanity in Yoruba cosmology. The Yoruba maintain a complex pantheon of orishas (spiritual forces), a sophisticated system of divination (Ifá), elaborate masquerade traditions, and the most urbanised pre-colonial societies in West Africa.
cultural_highlights: Ifá divination system — a UNESCO Intangible Heritage; a complete corpus of oral literature used for spiritual guidance, healing, and decision-making
Orisha worship — a pantheon of 401 spiritual forces, each with specific domains; the basis of Candomblé, Santería, and Trinidad Orisha
Gelede masquerade — elaborate masks performed to honour the 'Great Mothers' and celebrate feminine power
Bronze and terracotta art — Ife bronzes and terracottas (12th–15th century) are among the finest artworks ever produced in Africa
Àgbàdo (Agbada) garment — the flowing embroidered robe is a pan-West African symbol of dignity and celebration

food_traditions.name: Jollof rice
food_traditions.description: The most contested dish in West Africa — the Yoruba claim their party jollof is definitive; cooked in a rich tomato-pepper base with spices

food_traditions.name: Egusi soup
food_traditions.description: Ground melon seed soup with leafy vegetables and assorted proteins — served with pounded yam or eba

food_traditions.name: Amala and ewedu
food_traditions.description: Dark yam-flour swallow paired with jute leaf soup and gbegiri (bean soup) — the iconic Yoruba street food combination

food_traditions.name: Puff puff
food_traditions.description: Deep-fried sweet dough balls — the ubiquitous Yoruba party snack

music_traditions: Talking drum (dundun) — a pressure drum that mimics Yoruba tonal language, capable of conveying full sentences
Bata drums — sacred drums associated with Sango (deity of thunder), used in orisha ceremonies across the diaspora
Jùjú music — guitar-led genre pioneered by King Sunny Ade blending traditional Yoruba percussion with modern instruments
Afrobeats — the global genre with deep Yoruba roots in Lagos; artists like Fela Kuti, Burna Boy, and Wizkid trace this tradition
